quotes.verbatim_quote:
Now we come to Chords in the key of C, Dé and E+ Major. I do hope that you have followed my instructions to
the letter up until now, because if you don’t understand your lessons from one to seven, you surely won't under-
stand these lessons to follow.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Marks the shift to a second three-key cluster (C, D♭, E♭) while reinforcing that the new material is unintelligible without mastery of the prior key-set.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
First, we will work out Chord Exercises as we did in Lesson No. 2. These exercises are to be worked always up and
down the finger board, just as you have done with all of the other chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reapplies the chromatic up-and-down-the-neck drill as the default mechanism for absorbing any new chord shape.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Now then, any chord on a major mode can be used in place of the major triad.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the substitution axiom that licenses replacing every plain major chord with Cma7/Cma6/Cma9 voicings throughout the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
We don't want to strum a C major chord for the whole two bars, because it doesn’t have enough color, and be-
sides it sounds corny, so we add color by using progressions that are adaptable as follows:—
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates Baker's aesthetic motivation: static strumming is 'corny', motion through adaptable voicings is jazz.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The same thing is true with the minor and dominant chords.
Such as, if you have a Dmi chord (Any minor chord can be used on the minor mode) Dmi6, Dmi7 and Dmi9
are adaptable, because they are all on the same mode. With dominant chords there is no limit to what can be done.
For instance if you have a G7 Chord — you could use G13, G9, G7#5+9, G13#549, G75, G11, G1349 and so on.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the substitution rule from major to minor and dominant chords and enumerates the dominant-extension menu that drives all later progressions.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It all depends on how the chords are progressing. You just can’t throw these chords in any place, they have to be
part of some kind of progression.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Constrains the substitution licence: voicing choices are governed by voice-leading and progression, not free swap.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This can’t be done overnight. It took me years to figure them out, so it should take you at least two or three months
to learn them in the key of C major alone.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Sets the timescale expectation - months per key - that frames the whole book as long-term study rather than quick reference.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The circled number over each chord indicates which
(See Lesson 1)

chord is to be used.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Documents the numeric chord-reference system that ties every progression diagram back to the Lesson 1 catalog.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Practice each of these examples a dozen times a day.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Quantifies the daily drill load (twelve repetitions) Baker requires for the C-major chord-progressing material.
